_status: QUEUED FOR REVIEW (Proposal 0007, rev. 3). Not wired into the live interview. Shaped to drop into questionnaire_schema.js as a Decision & Risk depth module once 0007 is folded.
label: Decision rules
_flow: Per item: (1) surface — a concrete scenario draws out the rule; (2) reflect — the system phrases a first-person candidate back from the free-text answer; (3) endorse — the user accepts/edits/skips (endorse is the only write); (4) bound — the exception is captured in the same pass. Endorsing writes one standing_rules entry (kind:instruction) with the item's `binding` as `decision`. Skipping writes nothing. These are AUTHORED rules, not inferred signals — they carry no confidence score (§5). `priority` orders the injected Operating-rules block (lower first); hard lines get the lowest numbers.

fields.scenario: Free-text prompt that surfaces the rule without asking for it abstractly.
fields.reflect_template: How to render the user's answer back as a first-person candidate for endorsement. `{rule}` = extracted rule clause, `{exception}` = extracted boundary clause (may be empty); `{exception_suffix}` expands to ' (Exception: {exception})' or '' when empty.
fields.exception_probe: Shown if the answer carried no boundary; optional to fill.
fields.endorse: accept | edit | skip — only accept/edit writes.

questions.scenario: Name one thing you will NOT do when making a call, even under pressure to move fast.
questions.helper_text: The flat 'I don't / I won't' line. Example: 'I won't approve a recurring cost until the figure is confirmed in writing.'
questions.reflect_template: Proposed rule: "{rule}"{exception_suffix} — endorse, edit, or skip.
questions.exception_probe: Is there a case where this line does NOT apply? (Optional — leave blank if it's absolute.)

questions.scenario: When a request is underspecified or could mean two different things, what is your first move — before you do any of the work?
questions.helper_text: The default reflex on ambiguity. Example: 'I ask one clarifying question before acting.'
questions.reflect_template: Proposed rule: "{rule}"{exception_suffix} — endorse, edit, or skip.
questions.exception_probe: When do you skip that and just proceed instead? (Optional.)

questions.scenario: Think of a decision you cannot easily undo. What do you do differently than for one you can reverse?
questions.helper_text: The split between one-way and two-way doors. Example: 'On irreversible calls I slow down and seek more input; on reversible ones I move fast.'
questions.reflect_template: Proposed rule: "{rule}"{exception_suffix} — endorse, edit, or skip.
questions.exception_probe: Anything that overrides this — stakes, deadline, who's affected? (Optional.)

questions.scenario: When moving quickly and getting it exactly right pull against each other, which one gives — and is that always true?
questions.helper_text: The tradeoff under tension. The 'is that always true' half usually surfaces the exception in the same answer. Example: 'I trade speed for accuracy on consequential work, but move fast on small reversible stuff.'
questions.reflect_template: Proposed rule: "{rule}"{exception_suffix} — endorse, edit, or skip.
questions.exception_probe: Where does the opposite hold? (Optional — often the second half of your answer above.)

_example_transform.user_answer: I'd rather be slower and right, but not on small reversible stuff where speed is fine.
_example_transform.reflected_candidate: Proposed rule: "I trade speed for accuracy on consequential or irreversible decisions." (Exception: on small, reversible decisions I move fast and accept rough edges.) — endorse, edit, or skip.

on_endorse_writes.text: I trade speed for accuracy on consequential or irreversible decisions.
on_endorse_writes.exception: On small, reversible decisions I move fast and accept rough edges.
